Text: Still using nested loops? That's an instant interview fail.
Day 2 of turning you into a logic assassin.
Today's weapon. Two pointers.
You're given an array, sorted, and they ask for a pair that adds to a target.
Most people do this.
Loop inside a loop, brute force, slow, dead.
Here's the smarter way. Put one pointer at the start.
Put one pointer at the end.
Now watch closely. Add the two numbers.
If the sum is too small, move the left pointer right.
If the sum is too big, move the right pointer left.
If it matches, you're done.
One move, one decision, every step.
No guessing, no backtracking, no wasted work.
Each pointer moves only forward.
Never backward. That's why this works.
Time complexity drops from O of N squared to O of N.
One scan, perfect logic.
This pattern shows up everywhere.
Pair some, triplets, remove duplicates,
reverse arrays, palindrome checks, master this,
and brute force becomes illegal in your brain.
Two pointers, think less, move smarter, crack interviews.
